8. NonBayesian Parameter Estimation

When it is not natural to assign a prior, we treat the latent variable of interest as deterministic, but unknown. The observation model is not a distribution for conditioned on the latent variable, but rather a distribution for that is parameterized by this variable:

Let and denote the mean vector and covariance of as a function of . An estimate for the parameter in the family is valid if does not depend on .

Similar to our approach for nonBayesian hypothesis testing, one solution is to construct a minimax estimator:

(8.1)

[TODO] It turns out that solving for such minimax estimators is often less straightforward than is the case for hypothesis testing.

We instead analyze MSE. The reason MSE dominates in this nonBayesian setting is largely mathematical convenience

8.1. Bias and Covariance

The bias and covariance are now deterministic functions of :

(8.2)

In the nonBayesian case, we have , meaning the error and estimate are related by a constant, and the estimate covariance and the error covariance are the same for fixed . This is generally not true in the Bayesian case.

8.2. Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimators

Definition 8.1 (MVU Estimator). Let denote the set of valid and unbiased estimators. Consider a valid unbiased estimator for the parameter . If for every

(8.3)

then is the MVU estimator.

The MVU may not exist when is empty or no estimator in has a uniformly smaller variance than all others for all values of (see figure below). There is no systematic procedure for either determining whether an MVU estimator exists, or for computing it when it does exist.

.

ps4.2: improving estimators with a sufficient statistic!

8.3. Cramer-Rao Bound and Efficiency

Definition 8.2 (Parameter Score). Suppose is positive and differentiable on . Then

(8.4)

is referred to as the score for based on .

Interpretation: The score is the “best local direction” in the data for learning . If is large, then the observed data is pushing the likelihood toward larger . If the distribution changes sharply with , then the Fisher information is large and we can estimate more precisely.

Remark. There exist both data score and parameter score. The former has become more useful recently and the two are intimately related.

Theorem 8.1 (Cramer-Rao bound, scalar version). GIven sufficiently nice8 , if is unbiased and satisfies a regularity condition9 then

(8.5)

where is refer to as the Fisher information in about .

Proof

By hypothesis, we can treat and as two zero-mean random variables and apply Cauchy-Schwartz.

  • (by definition)
  • (since has zero mean)
  • comes from unbiasedness plus differentiating under the intgeral sign (justified by regularity conditions). So Cauchy schwartz becomes .

□

Corollary 8.1. Suppose in addition to the conditions of CRB above, is twice differentiable and satisfies the further regularity condition

(8.6)

Then the Fisher information can be equivalently expressed in the form

(8.7)

Interpreting the Regularity Conditions

  • Theorem 8.1 first regularity condition:

    • Note that

      (8.8)

      The first condition corresponds to

      (8.9)

      Which corresponds to requiring that the family of distributions be sufficiently well behaved such that the orders of integration (wrt ) and differentation (wrt ) can be interchanged.

  • Theorem 8.1 second regularity condition:

    • Likewise expresses that the orders of integration and differentation can be interchanged when is included in the integral, i.e.

      (8.10)

  • Corollary 8.1 additional regularity condition

    • Differentiate Equation 8.8 one more time, so that the condition is equivalent to exchanging the orders of integration and differentation as follows:

      (8.11)

Remark. Different versions of the Leibniz integral rule more generally establish different sufficient conditions under which the orders of integration and differentation can be exchanged. An example is the following:

Remarks

  • Implication: saturating the Cramer-Rao bound must be MVU. The converse is not true: the Cramer-Rao bound may not be tight and hence an MVU might not saturate it.
  • We can invoke the conditions for cauchy-schwartz saturation, see Corollary 8.2 below.
  • [TODO] CRB can be constructed for biased estimates but is not useful in practice. There is an analogous bound for random parameters, the Bayesian CRB.
  • For problems in which the regularity condition of CRB is not satisfied, then the Hammersley-Chapman-Robbin (HCR) bound applies.

Say an estimator is efficient if it saturates the CRB.

Corollary 8.2. An estimator is efficient if and only if it can be expressed in the form

(8.12)

where the RHS must be independent of for the estimator to be valid.

Proof

The Cauchy Schwartz saturation condition is , which gives the form . Plugging into CRB condition requires

□

Remarks

  • A valid estimator satisfying Equation 8.12 and the regularity condition is guaranteed to be unbiased.
  • The corollary implies that an efficient estimator is unique.
  • When it exists, an efficient estimator must be the unique MVU estimator for a problem.

8.4. Maximum Likelihood Estimation

(8.13)

when the right hand side exists and is unique. It is common to refer to as a likelihood function.

Corollary 8.3 (Efficient and ML coincide (if they exist)). Suppose for a model family that an efficient and maximum likelhood both exist. Then the two are identical.
Proof

Take the form of a valid, efficient estimator Equation 8.12 (RHS must not depend on ) and plug in . The score term is zero since it’s a maximum so we get

□

Remark. This works at other stationary points of the likelihood ratio (e.g. the minimum?). But it turns out that the likelihood function must be unimodal if there exists an efficient estimator by uniqueness.

Remark. Recall efficient means it saturates CRB! May not exist, and MVU also may not exist. When doesn’t exist, need not be good, and even it does, MVU doesn’t mean good.

Bayesian estimators don’t commute with invertible transformations but maximum likelihood does. The properties don’t necessarily get preserved, e.g. Jensen’s inequality.

Asymptotic Performance of MLE from 18.650

MLE is consistent (converges in probability to the true value of ). Proof uses argmax theorem ([TODO] prove) and Gibb’s inequality.

MLE is asymptotically normal. Use delta method and CLT.

MLE is asymptotically efficient (matches the Cramer Rao bound).
